faqs.question: What does 'Hu Tu Tu' mean in the chapter title?
faqs.answer: 'Hu Tu Tu' is one of the chanting sounds made in kabaddi! In different parts of India, the raider chants different words - 'kabaddi kabaddi' in the north, 'hu tu tu' in Maharashtra, and 'chaddi gudu' in the south. The raider must keep saying this without stopping or taking a new breath while trying to tag opponents. This chanting proves they have not taken a breath!

faqs.question: How is kabaddi played?
faqs.answer: Kabaddi is played between two teams on a flat ground divided into two halves. One player (the raider) goes to the other team's side and tries to touch as many opponents as possible. The raider must keep chanting 'kabaddi' in one breath and return to their side. If the raider is caught and cannot return, they are out. Each touched player earns a point for the raider's team. It is a game of speed, breath control, and quick thinking!

faqs.question: Why is kabaddi considered India's own game?
faqs.answer: Kabaddi originated in India thousands of years ago. It is mentioned in ancient Indian stories and was played by warriors to build strength and speed. It does not need any equipment - just players and an open ground. Every Indian state has its own name for the game. Today, India is the strongest kabaddi nation in the world and has won many international tournaments!

faqs.question: What is gilli-danda and how is it different from cricket?
faqs.answer: Gilli-danda uses two wooden sticks - a long one (danda, about 60 cm) and a short one (gilli, about 8 cm). The gilli is placed on the ground and flicked into the air by hitting its tip. Then the player hits it as far as possible. Like cricket, one side bats and the other fields, but gilli-danda needs no ball, no bat, no stumps - just two sticks! Many people believe cricket actually came from gilli-danda.

faqs.question: What traditional games can be played without any equipment?
faqs.answer: Many traditional games need no equipment at all! Hide and seek (chhupa-chhupi) just needs places to hide. Kabaddi needs only a flat ground. Chain-chain (a tagging game where tagged players join hands) needs nothing. Langdi (hopping on one foot to catch others) needs no equipment. Poshampa (a singing and catching game) is played with just two players making an arch. These free games prove that fun does not need to be bought!

faqs.question: What are some traditional games from different parts of India?
faqs.answer: India is rich in traditional games! North India has gilli-danda, pitthu (seven stones), and gulli-danda. Maharashtra has lagori and vithi-dandu. South India has pallanguzhi (a board game), goli (marbles), and nondi (hopscotch). East India has kabaddi (called ha-du-du in Bengal) and danguli. Punjab has pittu-garam and kikli (spinning while holding hands). Every region has unique games with different names!

faqs.question: Why are children playing fewer traditional games today?
faqs.answer: Today, many children prefer playing video games, watching TV, or using mobile phones. Families have moved to cities where there are fewer open grounds to play. Many children have busy schedules with homework and extra classes. Some children do not even know about traditional games! That is why it is important for schools and parents to teach these games so they do not disappear.

faqs.question: How do traditional games help children grow?
faqs.answer: Traditional games help children in many ways! Physically, they build strength, speed, flexibility, and stamina. Mentally, they teach strategy, quick thinking, and problem-solving. Socially, they teach teamwork, fair play, taking turns, and handling winning and losing gracefully. They also build confidence and leadership skills. Playing with friends creates lasting friendships and happy memories!

faqs.question: Is kabaddi played at the international level?
faqs.answer: Yes! Kabaddi has grown from a village game to an international sport. It has been part of the Asian Games since 1990, and India has won gold medals many times. The Pro Kabaddi League (PKL), started in 2014, made the sport very popular on TV. Kabaddi is now played in countries like Iran, South Korea, Japan, Kenya, and many more. India remains the world champion in kabaddi!

faqs.question: What games did our grandparents play when they were children?
faqs.answer: Our grandparents played many wonderful games! They played kabaddi and kho-kho in open fields, gilli-danda on village roads, marbles (kanche) in the mud, spinning tops (lattoo) with string, hopscotch (stapu) drawn with chalk, and pittu-garam with flat stones and a ball. In the evenings, they played board games like chaupar (ancient Ludo) and pallanguzhi. They had no screens but had endless fun with friends and simple things!
